rules.description: Namespace does not have Pod Security Admission enforce label
rules.risk: Without a pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce label, the namespace has no admission-level restrictions on pod security. Any pod spec, including privileged containers, will be admitted.
rules.remediation: Add label pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted (or baseline as minimum) to the namespace.

rules.description: Namespace PSA enforce level is set to privileged
rules.risk: The privileged Pod Security Standard allows all pod configurations including privileged containers, host namespaces, and host path mounts. This effectively disables all pod admission security checks.
rules.remediation: Change pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce to baseline or restricted.

rules.description: Secret object contains plaintext data in manifest file
rules.risk: Kubernetes Secret data is base64-encoded, not encrypted. Secrets stored in manifest files on disk are trivially decodable by anyone with filesystem access. This exposes credentials, tokens, and keys.
rules.remediation: Use SealedSecrets, ExternalSecrets, or a secrets management solution (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Do not store Secret manifests with data on disk.

rules.description: NetworkPolicy has an allow-all ingress rule
rules.risk: An empty ingress rule ({}) matches all sources, effectively allowing all inbound traffic. This renders the NetworkPolicy a no-op for ingress filtering.
rules.remediation: Specify explicit ingress rules with podSelector, namespaceSelector, or ipBlock restrictions.

rules.description: NetworkPolicy has an allow-all egress rule
rules.risk: An empty egress rule ({}) matches all destinations, allowing the pod to communicate with any endpoint including cloud metadata services, external C2 servers, and internal services like etcd.
rules.remediation: Specify explicit egress rules. At minimum, block access to cloud metadata (169.254.169.254) and internal control plane endpoints.

rules.risk: When failurePolicy is set to Ignore, the admission webhook is bypassed if it fails or is unreachable. An attacker can take the webhook offline (e.g., by deleting its service) to bypass admission controls and deploy malicious workloads.
rules.remediation: Set failurePolicy to Fail on all validating and mutating webhook configurations. Ensure the webhook backend is highly available.

rules.risk: PodSecurityPolicy was deprecated in Kubernetes 1.21 and removed in 1.25. Relying on PSP means the cluster uses an unsupported admission mechanism that will stop working after upgrade, leaving pods unprotected.
rules.remediation: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to Pod Security Admission (PSA) labels or a policy engine such as OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno.

rules.risk: A PersistentVolume with hostPath exposes a node directory to any pod that claims it. Unlike pod-level hostPath mounts, PVs can be claimed dynamically by any namespace, making it harder to audit which workloads access the host filesystem.
rules.remediation: Use a proper storage driver (CSI, NFS, cloud block storage) instead of hostPath for PersistentVolumes.
